it.full_brief:
# EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM)
## Regulatory Fact Document
### Regulation (EU) 2023/956, as amended by Regulation (EU) 2025/2083

---

# Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief covers Regulation (EU) 2023/956 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 10 May 2023 establishing a carbon border adjustment mechanism, as amended by Regulation (EU) 2025/2083 (the Omnibus Simplification Regulation, in force 20 October 2025). It synthesises the full enacted text of the primary regulation, the Commission's authoritative guidance portal (DG TAXUD), the EUR-Lex plain-language summary, the European Parliament Legislative Train tracking the 2025 amendment, the ICAP authoritative amendment analysis, and the DG TAXUD consolidated legislation and implementing acts registry.

**Convention notes:**
- "The workspace" denotes the freight forwarding operation and its supply-chain roles as described in the workspace profile. No company or individual is named.
- All binding obligations are either (a) verbatim FACT spans from the source text, or (b) labeled *Analytical inference:*, *Operational implication:*, or *Industry interpretation:*, or (c) routed to *Legal Confirmation Required:* callouts.
- Defined terms from the regulation are quoted verbatim from Article 3 where used.
- The brief reflects the definitive phase effective 1 January 2026, incorporating the 2025/2083 amendments. The transitional phase (1 October 2023–31 December 2025) is addressed in the timeline section for historical context only.
- **This brief does not constitute legal advice. All role-assignment conclusions are directed to *Legal Confirmation Required:* callouts.**

---

# What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

## What the regulation is

FACT: Regulation (EU) 2023/956 establishes "a carbon border adjustment mechanism (the 'CBAM') to address greenhouse gas emissions embedded in the goods listed in Annex I on their importation into the customs territory of the Union in order to prevent the risk of carbon leakage, thereby reducing global carbon emissions and supporting the goals of the Paris Agreement." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 1(1), European Parliament and Council, 10 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: "This Regulation applies to goods listed in Annex I originating in a third country, where those goods, or processed products from those goods resulting from the inward processing procedure referred to in Article 256 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013, are imported into the customs territory of the Union." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 2(1). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: The regulation "applies, during its initial transitional phase, to the following carbon-intensive imports: cement, electricity, fertilisers, iron and steel, aluminium and chemicals (hydrogen)." *Source: EUR-Lex Summary of Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Publications Office of the EU, last updated 6 February 2024. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/EN/legal-content/summary/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism.html

FACT: "CBAM will apply in its definitive regime from 2026, with a transitional phase of 2023 to 2025." *Source: European Commission DG TAXUD, Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (Official Hub). https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en

FACT: "The Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) successfully entered into force on the 1 January 2026, following a coordinated deployment across all EU Member States, integrating seamlessly the CBAM Registry with National Customs Import Systems, Taric and EU Customs Single Window." *Source: European Commission DG TAXUD, CBAM News — 14 January 2026. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en

## Why it applies to the workspace

*Legal Confirmation Required:* The regulation defines 'importer' as "either the person lodging a customs declaration for release for free circulation of goods in its own name and on its own behalf or, where the customs declaration is lodged by an indirect customs representative in accordance with Article 18 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013, the person on whose behalf such a declaration is lodged." (Article 3(15).) Whether the workspace, acting as freight forwarder, constitutes an 'importer' as defined, or is an 'indirect customs representative' as defined in the Union Customs Code on whose behalf declarations are lodged, requires legal determination against the workspace's specific contractual arrangements and customs filings.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* The regulation defines 'authorised CBAM declarant' as "a person authorised by a competent authority in accordance with Article 17" (Article 3(17)) and defines 'customs declarant' as "a declarant as defined in Article 5, point (15), of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013 lodging a customs declaration for release for free circulation of goods in its own name or the person in whose name such a declaration is lodged" (Article 3(16)). Whether the workspace occupies the 'authorised CBAM declarant' role — or whether its clients do, with the workspace acting as indirect customs representative — is a legal determination that turns on the workspace's contractual and operational arrangements in each transaction.

*Operational implication:* Freight forwarding operations that act as indirect customs representative for clients importing Annex I goods face the question of whether the CBAM declarant obligation attaches to the client (the importer of record) or to themselves. Article 5(2) of the regulation states: "Where an importer is not established in a Member State, the indirect customs representative shall submit the application for an authorisation." This makes the indirect customs representative's role CBAM-relevant in specific circumstances. *Source: Article 5(2), Regulation (EU) 2023/956. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Operational implication:* The workspace's cargo verticals — live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, humanitarian — do not in themselves correspond to Annex I goods (cement, iron and steel, aluminium, fertilisers, electricity, hydrogen). However, workspaces in a freight forwarding role also handle road cases, aluminium flight cases, steel structures, and staging components for events and film and TV production that may incorporate Annex I CN-coded materials. The workspace's exposure depends entirely on whether the CN codes of goods being imported match the Annex I table. Effect on specific cargo verticals requires CN-code-level classification review per shipment.

---

# Issues Requiring Immediate Action

## 1. WINDOW CLOSING — Authorised CBAM Declarant Application (if applicable)

FACT: "Goods shall be imported into the customs territory of the Union only by an authorised CBAM declarant." (Article 4.) FACT: "Any importer established in a Member State shall, prior to importing goods into the customs territory of the Union, apply for the status of authorised CBAM declarant." (Article 5(1).) The definitive phase started 1 January 2026. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Articles 4 and 5(1). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

**Action:** Legal counsel and operations leads must confirm, for every transaction type in which the workspace handles Annex I goods, which party holds the 'importer' and 'authorised CBAM declarant' status. Where the workspace is the indirect customs representative for a non-EU-established importer, the workspace may itself be required to hold authorised CBAM declarant status under Article 5(2). Applications are submitted via the CBAM Registry to the national competent authority of the EU Member State in which the applicant is established.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace, in transactions where it acts as indirect customs representative for non-EU clients importing Annex I goods, is legally required to hold authorised CBAM declarant status under Article 5(2), or whether this obligation falls on another party in the chain.

## 2. COST ALERT — Certificate Purchase Obligation (2027, covering 2026 imports)

FACT: "The start of CBAM certificates sales is postponed from 1 January 2026 to 1 February 2027. Declarants will acquire certificates in 2027 for embedded emissions from 2026 imports, with full compliance obligations applicable retroactively. Certificates prices for the 2026 compliance year will reflect the quarterly average of 2026 EU ETS allowance prices." *Source: ICAP, EU adopts simplifications of CBAM rules ahead of the compliance phase starting in 2026, 20 October 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/eu-adopts-simplifications-cbam-rules-ahead-compliance-phase-starting-2026

FACT: "The first CBAM certificate price is now available" — the Commission published the first quarterly price of CBAM certificates for 2026 Q1 on 7 April 2026. *Source: European Commission DG TAXUD, CBAM News — 7 April 2026. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en

**Action:** For every Annex I import since 1 January 2026 for which the workspace or its clients bear the authorised CBAM declarant obligation, the embedded emissions data must be tracked and preserved now, because the certificate purchase and surrender obligation will apply retroactively to all 2026 imports when certificates go on sale from February 2027. Failure to track now creates an irrecoverable data gap at surrender time.

## 3. ACTION REQUIRED — 50-Tonne De Minimis Threshold Assessment

FACT: Under Regulation (EU) 2025/2083 (in force 20 October 2025), "a new single mass-based threshold exempts importers that bring in 50 tons or less (cumulative net mass) of CBAM-covered goods into the EU per year." FACT: "Hydrogen and electricity are excluded from this 'de minimis' exemption." *Source: ICAP, EU adopts simplifications of CBAM rules ahead of the compliance phase starting in 2026, 20 October 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/eu-adopts-simplifications-cbam-rules-ahead-compliance-phase-starting-2026

**Action:** For each client or transaction type where Annex I goods are imported, establish the annual cumulative net mass figure. Clients or transactions below 50 tonnes cumulative annual net mass are exempt from the full authorisation and certificate regime (excluding hydrogen and electricity imports). This threshold is subject to annual Commission review. *Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the 50-tonne threshold is calculated per importer entity or per declarant entity, and whether aggregation rules across multiple CN codes apply in a specific manner, requires legal confirmation against the full text of Regulation (EU) 2025/2083.

## 4. ACTION REQUIRED — Annual Declaration Deadline (New: 30 September)

FACT: "The submission deadline for the annual CBAM declaration is postponed to 30 September (previously 31 May) of the year following importation of covered goods." *Source: ICAP, EU adopts simplifications of CBAM rules ahead of the compliance phase starting in 2026, 20 October 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/eu-adopts-simplifications-cbam-rules-ahead-compliance-phase-starting-2026

FACT (original regulation): "By 31 May of each year, and for the first time in 2027 for the year 2026, each authorised CBAM declarant shall use the CBAM registry referred to in Article 14 to submit a CBAM declaration for the preceding calendar year." (Article 6(1).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 6(1). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the Regulation (EU) 2025/2083 amendment that changed the deadline to 30 September supersedes Article 6(1)'s 31 May deadline for the first declaration covering 2026 imports must be confirmed against the full text of Regulation (EU) 2025/2083. The ICAP source records 30 September as the amended deadline.

**Action:** Build the 30 September declaration deadline (year following importation) into trade compliance calendars starting with the first declaration for calendar year 2026, due 30 September 2027.

## 5. COST ALERT — Penalty Exposure for Non-Compliance

FACT: "An authorised CBAM declarant who fails to surrender, by 31 May of each year [as amended, 30 September], the number of CBAM certificates that corresponds to the emissions embedded in goods imported during the preceding calendar year shall be held liable for the payment of a penalty. Such a penalty shall be identical to the excess emissions penalty set out in Article 16(3) of Directive 2003/87/EC and increased pursuant to Article 16(4) of that Directive, applicable in the year of importation of the goods. Such a penalty shall apply for each CBAM certificate that the authorised CBAM declarant has not surrendered." (Article 26(1).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 26(1). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: "Where a person other than an authorised CBAM declarant introduces goods into the customs territory of the Union without complying with the obligations under this Regulation, that person shall be held liable for the payment of a penalty … an amount from three to five times the penalty referred to in paragraph 1, applicable in the year of introduction of the goods, for each CBAM certificate that the person has not surrendered." (Article 26(2).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 26(2). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Specific current EU ETS excess emissions penalty rate not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-27 — the penalty is cross-referenced to Article 16(3) and (4) of Directive 2003/87/EC and increases annually with the European Index of Consumer Prices. Legal counsel should confirm the applicable rate for the year of importation.*

FACT: "The payment of the penalty shall not release the authorised CBAM declarant from the obligation to surrender the outstanding number of CBAM certificates in a given year." (Article 26(3).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 26(3). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

---

# How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

## Supply chain roles defined in the regulation

FACT: 'importer' is defined as "either the person lodging a customs declaration for release for free circulation of goods in its own name and on its own behalf or, where the customs declaration is lodged by an indirect customs representative in accordance with Article 18 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013, the person on whose behalf such a declaration is lodged." (Article 3(15).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 3(15). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: 'authorised CBAM declarant' is defined as "a person authorised by a competent authority in accordance with Article 17." (Article 3(17).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 3(17). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: 'operator' is defined as "any person who operates or controls an installation in a third country." (Article 3(31).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 3(31). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

## Role placement rules

FACT: Article 5(1): "Any importer established in a Member State shall, prior to importing goods into the customs territory of the Union, apply for the status of authorised CBAM declarant. Where such an importer appoints an indirect customs representative in accordance with Article 18 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013 and the indirect customs representative agrees to act as an authorised CBAM declarant, the indirect customs representative shall submit the application for an authorisation." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 5(1). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: Article 5(2): "Where an importer is not established in a Member State, the indirect customs representative shall submit the application for an authorisation." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 5(2). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: "During the transitional period … Where the importer is not established in a Member State, the reporting obligations shall apply to the indirect customs representative." (Article 32.) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 32. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: Authorised CBAM declarants may delegate submission of CBAM declarations to a third party, under Regulation (EU) 2025/2083, "while retaining responsibility. The third party must be established in an EU member state and hold an Economic Operators Registration and Identification (EORI) number." *Source: ICAP, EU adopts simplifications of CBAM rules ahead of the compliance phase starting in 2026, 20 October 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/eu-adopts-simplifications-cbam-rules-ahead-compliance-phase-starting-2026

## Role placement requiring legal confirmation

*Legal Confirmation Required:* For each transaction type the workspace executes (acting as direct agent, indirect customs representative, or freight forwarder on behalf of EU-established importers vs. non-EU-established importers), legal counsel must determine: (a) which party is the 'importer' as defined in Article 3(15); (b) whether the workspace is an 'indirect customs representative' in that transaction; (c) whether, under Article 5(1) or 5(2), the authorised CBAM declarant obligation attaches to the workspace or to its client; and (d) whether any delegation of declaration submission to the workspace as third party is in scope and what liability retention attaches.

*Operational implication:* For air-priority transactions moving Annex I goods (e.g., aluminium components for staging or automotive parts) from third countries into the EU customs territory, the workspace's standard customs filing role determines which compliance path applies. The distinction between direct representation (client retains importer status) and indirect representation (workspace becomes the named declarant entity under UCC Article 18) is the pivotal legal question per transaction.

---

# Authoritative Guidance Document Analysis

## Commission Implementing Acts — Definitive Phase (December 2025 Package)

FACT: The DG TAXUD consolidated legislation page records the following implementing acts for the definitive phase, all dated December 2025:
- "Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2546 of 10 December 2025 … on the application of the principles for verification of declared embedded emissions"
- "Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2547 of 10 December 2025 … as regards the methods for the calculation of emissions embedded in goods"
- "Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2548 of 10 December 2025 … as regards the calculation and publication of the price of CBAM certificates"
- "Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2619 of 16 December 2025 … as regards the information communicated by customs authorities"
- "Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2620 of 16 December 2025 … as regards the calculation of the free allocation adjustment to the number of CBAM certificates to be surrendered"
- "Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2621 of 16 December 2025 … as regards the establishment of default values"

*Source: European Commission DG TAXUD, CBAM Legislation and Guidance. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism/cbam-legislation-and-guidance_en

*Operational implication:* These implementing acts govern how embedded emissions are actually calculated, what default values apply per sector/country, how certificate prices are published, how customs data flows to the Commission, and how free allocation adjustments reduce the certificate surrender number. Workspaces in an authorised CBAM declarant role (or advising clients who are) need to ensure their emission data-gathering aligns with Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2547 and their calculation of certificates due aligns with 2025/2620 and 2025/2621. These texts are not reproduced in full in the source blocks; their full text is available at the DG TAXUD legislation page.

## Guidelines for Operational Procedures (1 January 2026)

FACT: The Commission published "CBAM Goes Live: Guidelines for Operational Procedures for 1 January 2026" on 19 March 2026, available via the DG TAXUD hub. *Source: European Commission DG TAXUD, CBAM News — 19 March 2026. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en

*Operational implication:* This guidance document is the primary operational reference for the definitive phase. Workspaces and their compliance teams should obtain and read this document in full. It is linked from the DG TAXUD hub but its full text is not reproduced in the source blocks; citations from it must be obtained from the document directly before relying on them in client conversations.

## FAQs (Updated May 2026)

FACT: "The European Commission has published the updated Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) for the definitive period" on 27 May 2026. *Source: European Commission DG TAXUD, CBAM News — 27 May 2026. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en

*Operational implication:* The Commission FAQ is a Tier 2 regulator guidance document. It is the appropriate reference for operational questions before seeking legal opinion. The full FAQ text is not reproduced in the source blocks.

## Default Values and Benchmarks

FACT: "The Commission has published the default values and the benchmarks as Excel files" on 13 February 2026. "The legally binding values are set out in Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2621." The benchmarks "are set out in Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2620." *Source: European Commission DG TAXUD, CBAM Legislation and Guidance. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism/cbam-legislation-and-guidance_en

*Operational implication:* Where an authorised CBAM declarant cannot obtain actual verified emissions data from the third-country producer, these default values apply. Default values carry a mark-up above average emission intensity to preserve environmental integrity. Using default values rather than actual verified emissions will therefore generally result in a higher certificate surrender obligation. This affects cost estimation for clients importing Annex I goods from producers who are not registered in the CBAM Registry or who cannot supply verified data.

---

# Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

## MONITORING — Scope Extension to Downstream Goods (2026–2027)

FACT: The DG TAXUD hub records: "Commission welcomes Council agreement on strengthening CBAM — The European Commission welcomes the Council agreement to extend the CBAM to specific downstream goods and reinforce existing anti-circumvention safeguards" (12 June 2026). *Source: European Commission DG TAXUD, CBAM News — 12 June 2026. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en

FACT: The DG TAXUD legislation page lists: "Proposal for a REGULATION OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL amending Regulation (EU) 2023/956 as regards the extension of its scope to downstream goods and anti-circumvention measures" with an accompanying Impact Assessment Report and Executive Summary. *Source: European Commission DG TAXUD, CBAM Legislation and Guidance. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism/cbam-legislation-and-guidance_en

*Analytical inference:* The Council agreement of 12 June 2026 on downstream goods extension indicates the scope of CBAM is expanding beyond the current six sectors. Downstream goods include products further along the value chain from Annex I materials — for example, structural steel components, fabricated aluminium assemblies, and fertiliser-derived chemicals. For workspaces handling high-value automotive, live events staging equipment, or film and TV production structures incorporating steel or aluminium, downstream goods expansion could bring currently-out-of-scope items into CBAM coverage. The re-check window for this development is Q4 2026, when the formal adoption timeline should be clearer.

**Re-check window: Q4 2026** — monitor for adoption of the downstream goods extension regulation and its effective date.

## MONITORING — Implementing Act on Carbon Price Paid in Third Countries

FACT: "On 13 May 2026, the Commission published the implementing act for a 4-week public consultation ending on 10 June 2026. The publication follows a call for evidence whose synopsis report is also available." *Source: European Commission DG TAXUD, CBAM News — 13 May 2026. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en

*Analytical inference:* This implementing act will govern how carbon prices already paid in third countries (e.g., China's ETS, South Korea's ETS) are converted into certificate deductions. For workspaces whose clients import from countries with active carbon pricing, this act determines the financial value of the deduction. A finalised implementing act is expected in H2 2026.

**Re-check window: H2 2026** — monitor for adoption and publication in the Official Journal.

## MONITORING — Common Central Platform (Certificate Sales)

FACT: "New deadline announced for CBAM CCP Call for Tenders — The new deadline for submissions is Monday, 6 April 2026 at 5:00 PM (CET)" for the common central platform procurement. *Source: European Commission DG TAXUD, CBAM News — 13 March 2026. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en

*Analytical inference:* The Common Central Platform through which CBAM certificates are sold is still in procurement as of early April 2026, with certificates sales starting February 2027. Operational readiness of the platform is a prerequisite for certificate purchase. The workspace and its clients should monitor DG TAXUD news for platform deployment confirmation before Q4 2026.

**Re-check window: Q3 2026.**

## MONITORING — Indirect Emissions Technical Study

FACT: "Technical Study on Indirect Emissions in the CBAM, including annexes, now available on the Publications Office of the European Union website" was published 8 June 2026. The study "addresses three questions: 1) how to determine operational default emission factors for indirect emissions; 2) under what conditions declarants should be allowed to claim actual indirect emissions… and 3) whether and how indirect emissions coverage could be extended to additional CBAM sectors." *Source: European Commission DG TAXUD, CBAM News — 8 June 2026. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en

*Analytical inference:* This study is pre-legislative policy analysis feeding future implementing acts on indirect emissions. Extended indirect emissions coverage could increase the certificate surrender obligation for aluminium and iron/steel goods (currently Annex II, direct-only). Re-check window: H1 2027 for any resulting implementing act proposal.

---

# Threshold Questions

## Q1: Do the goods handled by the workspace fall under Annex I?

**Status: Requires CN-code classification review per shipment — cannot be decided generically.**

FACT: The regulation "applies to goods listed in Annex I originating in a third country." (Article 2(1).) Annex I lists goods by Combined Nomenclature (CN) code. The primary CN code categories are: cement (CN 2507, 2523), electricity (CN 2716), fertilisers (CN 2808, 2814, 2834, 3102, 3105 except 3105 60 00), iron and steel (CN Chapter 72 with specific exclusions, CN 2601, 7301–7318, 7326), aluminium (CN 7601–7616), and hydrogen (CN 2804 10 00). *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Annex I. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Operational implication:* The workspace's primary cargo verticals (live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, humanitarian) do not as verticals map to Annex I. However, individual shipments within these verticals may contain goods classified under iron and steel (CN 72xx) or aluminium (CN 76xx) CN codes — for example, aluminium flight cases, steel truss structures, or automotive aluminium components. Each import shipment requires individual CN code assessment.

## Q2: Does the 50-tonne de minimis threshold apply?

**Status: Partially decided — threshold is 50 tonnes cumulative net mass per year (excluding hydrogen and electricity); annual review by Commission.**

FACT: "A new single mass-based threshold exempts importers that bring in 50 tons or less (cumulative net mass) of CBAM-covered goods into the EU per year. Hydrogen and electricity are excluded from this 'de minimis' exemption. The threshold may be recalculated if emission intensities or trade patterns change, based on an annual review by the Commission." *Source: ICAP, EU adopts simplifications of CBAM rules ahead of the compliance phase starting in 2026, 20 October 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/eu-adopts-simplifications-cbam-rules-ahead-compliance-phase-starting-2026

FACT (original regulation): "This Regulation shall not apply to: (a) goods listed in Annex I to this Regulation which are imported into the customs territory of the Union provided that the intrinsic value of such goods does not exceed, per consignment, the value specified for goods of negligible value as referred to in Article 23 of Council Regulation (EC) No 1186/2009." (Article 2(3)(a).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 2(3)(a). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the 2025/2083 mass-based threshold replaces or supplements the original intrinsic-value de minimis in Article 2(3)(a) requires confirmation against the full text of Regulation (EU) 2025/2083.

FACT: Anti-circumvention provision: "Artificially splitting shipments into consignments the intrinsic value of which does not exceed the threshold referred to in Article 2(3)" constitutes a practice of circumvention. (Article 27(2)(b).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 27(2)(b). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

## Q3: Is the country of origin exempt?

**Status: Partially decided — EFTA/EEA countries and certain territories are listed in Annex III.**

FACT: "This Regulation shall not apply to goods originating in the following countries: Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, Switzerland" and "the following territories: Büsingen, Heligoland, Livigno, Ceuta, Melilla." (Annex III.) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Annex III. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: "This Regulation applies to goods listed in Annex I to this Regulation originating in a third country." FACT: "'Importation' means release for free circulation as provided for in Article 201 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013." (Article 3(4).) "Imported goods shall be considered as originating in third countries in accordance with the rules for non-preferential origin as referred to in Article 59 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013." (Article 2(5).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Articles 2(5) and 3(4). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

## Q4: Direct or indirect emissions — which apply to the goods in question?

**Status: Decided by Annex II.**

FACT: "For goods listed in Annex II only direct emissions shall be calculated and taken into account." (Article 7(1).) Annex II lists iron and steel (same CN codes as Annex I), aluminium (same CN codes as Annex I), and hydrogen (CN 2804 10 00). *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 7(1) and Annex II. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Operational implication:* For cement and fertilisers (not in Annex II), both direct and indirect emissions are in scope. For iron/steel, aluminium, and hydrogen, only direct emissions are counted in the current regime. This affects how the workspace advises clients on emission data requirements from their suppliers.

## Q5: Is the goods definition 'simple' or 'complex'?

**Status: Decided by Annex IV definitions.**

FACT: Annex IV defines: "'simple goods' means goods produced in a production process requiring exclusively input materials (precursors) and fuels having zero embedded emissions" and "'complex goods' means goods other than simple goods." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Annex IV, point 1. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Operational implication:* Most fabricated steel and aluminium products that may enter the workspace's supply chain (e.g., staging structures, automotive components) are likely 'complex goods' because they incorporate precursors with embedded emissions. The calculation methodology for complex goods is more data-intensive. *Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether specific goods handled in the workspace's transactions qualify as 'simple' or 'complex' under Annex IV requires technical review against the definition and the system boundaries set in Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2547.

# Substantive Requirements

## S1. Annual CBAM Declaration

FACT: "By 31 May of each year, and for the first time in 2027 for the year 2026, each authorised CBAM declarant shall use the CBAM registry referred to in Article 14 to submit a CBAM declaration for the preceding calendar year." (Article 6(1).) As amended by Regulation (EU) 2025/2083, the deadline is 30 September. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 6(1). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956; ICAP source for amendment. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/eu-adopts-simplifications-cbam-rules-ahead-compliance-phase-starting-2026

FACT: The declaration contains: "(a) the total quantity of each type of goods imported during the preceding calendar year, expressed in megawatt-hours for electricity and in tonnes for other goods; (b) the total embedded emissions in the goods … expressed in … tonnes of CO2e emissions per tonne of each type of goods, calculated in accordance with Article 7 and verified in accordance with Article 8; (c) the total number of CBAM certificates to be surrendered … after the reduction that is due on the account of the carbon price paid in a country of origin in accordance with Article 9 and the adjustment necessary to reflect the extent to which EU ETS allowances are allocated free of charge in accordance with Article 31; (d) copies of verification reports, issued by accredited verifiers." (Article 6(2).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 6(2). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

## S2. Certificate Surrender

FACT: "By 31 May of each year, and for the first time in 2027 for the year 2026, the authorised CBAM declarant shall surrender via the CBAM registry a number of CBAM certificates that corresponds to the embedded emissions declared in accordance with Article 6(2), point (c), and verified in accordance with Article 8." (Article 22(1).) As amended by Regulation (EU) 2025/2083, the surrender deadline aligns with the new 30 September declaration deadline. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 22(1). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

## S3. Quarterly Holding Requirement

FACT (as amended by Regulation (EU) 2025/2083): "The requirement for declarants to hold a number of CBAM certificates on their registry account at the end of each quarter is reduced to 50% of embedded emissions in goods imported since the beginning of the calendar year (down from 80%)." *Source: ICAP, EU adopts simplifications of CBAM rules ahead of the compliance phase starting in 2026, 20 October 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/eu-adopts-simplifications-cbam-rules-ahead-compliance-phase-starting-2026

FACT (original regulation, pre-amendment): "The authorised CBAM declarant shall ensure that the number of CBAM certificates on its account in the CBAM registry at the end of each quarter corresponds to at least 80 % of the embedded emissions, determined by reference to default values in accordance with the methods set out in Annex IV, in all goods it has imported since the beginning of the calendar year." (Article 22(2).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 22(2). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Operational implication:* The 50% quarterly requirement (post-amendment) means the workspace or its clients must plan certificate purchases during the calendar year, not only at surrender time. The calculation basis uses default values, not actual verified emissions, for the quarterly check.

## S4. Certificate Price Mechanism

FACT: "The Commission shall calculate the price of CBAM certificates as the average of the closing prices of EU ETS allowances on the auction platform … for each calendar week." (Article 21(1).) FACT (as amended by Regulation (EU) 2025/2083): "Certificates prices for the 2026 compliance year will reflect the quarterly average of 2026 EU ETS allowance prices. Thereafter, CBAM certificates can be purchased any time and prices will reflect the weekly average of EU ETS auction closing prices." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 21(1); ICAP amendment analysis. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956 and https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/eu-adopts-simplifications-cbam-rules-ahead-compliance-phase-starting-2026

FACT: "The first CBAM certificate price is now available" — the Commission published the first quarterly price of CBAM certificates for 2026 Q1 on 7 April 2026. *Source: European Commission DG TAXUD, CBAM News — 7 April 2026. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en

## S5. Verification Requirement

FACT: "The authorised CBAM declarant shall ensure that the total embedded emissions declared in the CBAM declaration submitted pursuant to Article 6 are verified by a verifier accredited pursuant to Article 18, based on the verification principles set out in Annex VI." (Article 8(1).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 8(1). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: "Any person accredited in accordance with Implementing Regulation (EU) 2018/2067 for a relevant group of activities shall be an accredited verifier for the purpose of this Regulation." (Article 18(1).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 18(1). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: Verification principles (Annex VI) state: "verifiers shall carry out verifications with an attitude of professional scepticism; the total embedded emissions to be declared in the CBAM declaration shall be considered as verified only if the verifier finds with reasonable assurance that the verification report is free of material misstatements and of material non-conformities regarding the calculation of embedded emissions in accordance with the rules of Annex IV; installation visits by the verifier shall be mandatory except where specific criteria for waiving the installation visit are met." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Annex VI, point 1. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Operational implication:* Commissioning accredited verifiers for each producer installation in the supply chain is a prerequisite for the annual declaration. This is a supply-chain data and relationship task, not solely an administrative filing task. For the workspace's clients, this may require contractual terms with third-country suppliers requiring them to register in the CBAM Registry, maintain their own emissions records under Annex V, and facilitate verifier access.

## S6. Record-Keeping

FACT: "The authorised CBAM declarant shall keep records of the information required to calculate the embedded emissions in accordance with the requirements laid down in Annex V. Those records shall be sufficiently detailed to enable verifiers accredited pursuant to Article 18 to verify the embedded emissions in accordance with Article 8 and Annex VI and to enable the Commission and the competent authority to review the CBAM declaration in accordance with Article 19(2)." (Article 7(5).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 7(5). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: "The authorised CBAM declarant shall keep those records of information referred to in paragraph 5, including the report of the verifier, until the end of the fourth year after the year in which the CBAM declaration has been or should have been submitted." (Article 7(6).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 7(6). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

## S7. Carbon Price Deduction

FACT: "An authorised CBAM declarant may claim in the CBAM declaration a reduction in the number of CBAM certificates to be surrendered in order to take into account the carbon price paid in the country of origin for the declared embedded emissions. The reduction may be claimed only if the carbon price has been effectively paid in the country of origin. In such a case, any rebate or other form of compensation available in that country that would have resulted in a reduction of that carbon price shall be taken into account." (Article 9(1).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 9(1). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT (as amended by Regulation (EU) 2025/2083): "Standard annual average carbon prices, or default carbon prices, may be used for countries where carbon pricing mechanisms are in place, but an actual carbon price paid cannot be established. From 2027, the Commission will publish default carbon prices and related calculation methodologies in the CBAM registry." FACT (amendment): "Carbon prices paid in a third country other than the country of origin of the imported goods are also eligible for deduction, if corresponding evidence can be provided." *Source: ICAP, EU adopts simplifications of CBAM rules ahead of the compliance phase starting in 2026, 20 October 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/eu-adopts-simplifications-cbam-rules-ahead-compliance-phase-starting-2026

FACT: The documentation verifying carbon price paid "shall be certified by a person that is independent from the authorised CBAM declarant and from the authorities of the country of origin." (Article 9(2).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 9(2). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

## S8. Free Allocation Adjustment

FACT: "The CBAM certificates to be surrendered in accordance with Article 22 of this Regulation shall be adjusted to reflect the extent to which EU ETS allowances are allocated free of charge in accordance with Article 10a of Directive 2003/87/EC to installations producing, within the Union, the goods listed in Annex I to this Regulation." (Article 31(1).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 31(1). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT (amendment): "The calculation of the embedded emissions takes into account an adjustment factor reflecting the sectoral free allocation of allowances that is still provided to EU producers (in line with the gradual phase-out of free allocation for CBAM-covered sectors in the EU ETS from 2026 to 2034)." *Source: ICAP, EU adopts simplifications of CBAM rules ahead of the compliance phase starting in 2026, 20 October 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/eu-adopts-simplifications-cbam-rules-ahead-compliance-phase-starting-2026

*Analytical inference:* As free allocation phases out between 2026 and 2034, the adjustment factor decreases, meaning the effective number of CBAM certificates to be surrendered increases over time for the same volume of goods. Clients importing Annex I goods should model the cost trajectory across this 2026–2034 window, not only the current year.

## S9. Repurchase of Excess Certificates

FACT: "The number of certificates subject to repurchase … shall be limited to one third of the total number of CBAM certificates purchased by the authorised CBAM declarant during the previous calendar year." (Article 23(2).) FACT: "The repurchase price for each CBAM certificate shall be the price paid by the authorised CBAM declarant for that certificate at the time of purchase." (Article 23(3).) FACT: "The authorised CBAM declarant shall submit the repurchase request by 30 June of each year during which CBAM certificates were surrendered." (Article 23(1).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 23. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

## S10. Annual Certificate Cancellation

FACT: "On 1 July of each year, the Commission shall cancel any CBAM certificates that were purchased during the year before the previous calendar year and that remained in the account of an authorised CBAM declarant in the CBAM registry. Those CBAM certificates shall be cancelled without any compensation." (Article 24.) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 24. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Operational implication:* Certificates have limited validity. Purchasing certificates in advance of need creates financial risk: certificates purchased in year N that remain unused through year N+1 are cancelled on 1 July of year N+2 without compensation. Certificate purchase timing must be managed within the repurchase-up-to-one-third and cancellation rules.

# Product-Specific Compliance Status

The workspace's primary cargo verticals (live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, humanitarian) do not align with CBAM's currently covered sectors at the vertical level. However, specific product categories within these verticals may contain Annex I materials:

**Iron and steel (CN Chapter 72 and downstream CN codes 7301–7318, 7326):** Steel trussing and staging for live events and film sets; automotive steel components. FACT: CN Chapter 72 (iron and steel) is covered under Annex I with specific exclusions for certain ferro-alloys (7202 2x) and ferrous waste/scrap (7204). *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Annex I. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

**Aluminium (CN 7601–7616):** Aluminium flight cases and equipment cases for live events, film, and luxury goods transport; automotive aluminium parts. FACT: "Aluminium products should be included in the CBAM as they are highly exposed to carbon leakage. Moreover, in several industrial applications they are in direct competition with steel products." (Recital 36.) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Recital 36. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Operational implication:* For aluminium and iron/steel goods under Annex II: "only direct emissions shall be calculated and taken into account." This means for these product categories, indirect emissions (electricity used in production) are excluded from the current CBAM calculation, simplifying the data requirement.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether specific products handled by the workspace within these verticals (e.g., an aluminium road case, a structural steel rigging component, an automotive body panel) are classified under the applicable Annex I CN codes requires CN tariff classification advice per product type, which is a customs law determination, not a factual assertion this brief can make.

# Registration and Reporting Obligations

## Authorised CBAM Declarant Registration

FACT: "The application for an authorisation shall be submitted via the CBAM registry established in accordance with Article 14." (Article 5(3).) FACT: "Access to the CBAM registry should be requested through the National Competent Authority (NCA) of the EU country in which the importer is established." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 5(3); DG TAXUD hub. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956 and https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en

FACT: The application information includes: "(a) name, address and contact information; (b) EORI number; (c) main economic activity carried out in the Union; (d) certification by the tax authority … that the applicant is not subject to an outstanding recovery order for national tax debts; (e) declaration of honour that the applicant was not involved in any serious infringements or repeated infringements of customs legislation, taxation rules or market abuse rules during the five years preceding the year of the application … (f) information necessary to demonstrate the applicant's financial and operational capacity … (g) estimated monetary value and volume of imports of goods … for the calendar year during which the application is submitted, and for the following calendar year; (h) names and contact information of the persons on behalf of whom the applicant is acting, if applicable." (Article 5(5).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 5(5). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: A guarantee is required "if the applicant was not established throughout the two financial years preceding the year when the application … was submitted. The competent authority shall fix the amount of such guarantee at the amount, calculated as the aggregate value of the number of CBAM certificates that the authorised CBAM declarant would have to surrender." The guarantee "shall be a bank guarantee, payable at first demand, by a financial institution operating in the Union or another form of guarantee which provides equivalent assurance." (Article 17(5).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 17(5). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

## Annual CBAM Declaration

FACT: First declaration for year 2026 is due by 30 September 2027 (as amended; original 31 May 2027). *Source: Article 6(1), Regulation (EU) 2023/956 and ICAP amendment analysis. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

## Delegated Reporting (Post-2025/2083 Amendment)

FACT (as amended): "Authorized CBAM declarants may delegate submission of CBAM declarations to a third party, while retaining responsibility. The third party must be established in an EU member state and hold an Economic Operators Registration and Identification (EORI) number." *Source: ICAP, EU adopts simplifications of CBAM rules ahead of the compliance phase starting in 2026, 20 October 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/eu-adopts-simplifications-cbam-rules-ahead-compliance-phase-starting-2026

*Operational implication:* The workspace may be engaged by clients to act as a delegated third party for CBAM declaration submission, without itself being the authorised CBAM declarant. This is a new service opportunity and a new liability exposure — the workspace would need its own EORI number (standard for a freight forwarder) and would need contractual clarity on liability allocation, since responsibility remains with the authorised CBAM declarant.

## Commission Review Period

FACT: "The Commission may review CBAM declarations … within the period ending with the fourth year after the year during which the CBAM declarations should have been submitted." (Article 19(2).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 19(2). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

# Operational System Requirements

## 1. CN Code Classification and Screening System

**Scope:** All import shipments into the EU customs territory.
**Deadline:** Operational from 1 January 2026 (definitive phase active).
**Gap from current operational baseline:** Freight forwarding operations typically classify goods for customs duty purposes; CBAM requires the same CN code to also be screened against Annex I. This is an overlay on existing customs classification workflows, not a separate system, but requires staff awareness and process documentation.

*Operational implication:* For every shipment the workspace handles as customs agent or indirect customs representative for EU imports, a CBAM Annex I screen should be applied at booking/classification stage.

## 2. Embedded Emissions Data Collection Infrastructure

**Scope:** All Annex I goods imports where the workspace or its clients bear the authorised CBAM declarant obligation.
**Deadline:** Data must be collected for all 2026 imports before the September 2027 declaration date.
**Gap from current operational baseline:** Standard freight forwarding does not collect embedded emissions data from producers. This requires a supplier data-request process, use of the CBAM Registry operator registration system, or reliance on default values (with higher cost implications).

FACT: Annex V minimum data requirements include: identification of the authorised CBAM declarant (name, CBAM account number); type and quantity of goods; country of origin; actual emissions or default values; and, for actual emissions, identification of the installation, contact information of the operator, verification reports, and specific embedded emissions. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Annex V. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: Records must be retained "until the end of the fourth year after the year in which the CBAM declaration has been or should have been submitted." (Article 7(6).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 7(6). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

## 3. Accredited Verifier Engagement

**Scope:** All actual-emission-based CBAM declarations.
**Deadline:** Verification must be complete before declaration submission.
**Gap:** Workspaces and their clients must identify, contract, and brief accredited verifiers. The verifier must be accredited under Implementing Regulation (EU) 2018/2067 or the new Delegated Regulation (EU) 2025/2551 on accreditation. Verifier capacity in the EU market is a known constraint as CBAM scales. *Source: Article 18(1) and Delegated Regulation (EU) 2025/2551 listing on DG TAXUD legislation page. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

## 4. CBAM Registry Account and Certificate Management

**Scope:** All authorised CBAM declarants.
**Deadline:** Account required before importing Annex I goods; certificate purchases from February 2027.
**Gap:** The Common Central Platform for certificate sales was still in procurement as of April 2026. The workspace or its clients need a CBAM Registry account number, EORI number, and National Competent Authority relationship before importing.

## 5. Contract Clauses with Third-Country Suppliers

**Scope:** Supply agreements for clients importing Annex I goods from non-EU producers.
**Gap:** Standard trade and freight contracts do not address CBAM data obligations. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether the workspace's client agreements should include clauses requiring suppliers to: (a) register in the CBAM Registry as operators; (b) maintain Annex V records; (c) provide verified emissions data; and (d) notify of carbon price payments in the country of origin — is a commercial and legal drafting question requiring counsel.

# Exemptions and Edge Cases

## De Minimis Threshold (Post-2025/2083)

FACT: "A new single mass-based threshold exempts importers that bring in 50 tons or less (cumulative net mass) of CBAM-covered goods into the EU per year. Hydrogen and electricity are excluded from this 'de minimis' exemption. The threshold may be recalculated if emission intensities or trade patterns change, based on an annual review by the Commission." *Source: ICAP, EU adopts simplifications of CBAM rules ahead of the compliance phase starting in 2026, 20 October 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/eu-adopts-simplifications-cbam-rules-ahead-compliance-phase-starting-2026

*Analytical inference:* For the workspace's cargo verticals, individual shipments of aluminium or steel goods are frequently low-mass high-value (e.g., precision automotive parts, event equipment). Many individual clients may fall below the 50-tonne annual threshold. However, the threshold is cumulative per importer per year, not per shipment. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether aggregation is per legal entity importer or per EORI number requires legal confirmation against the full text of Regulation (EU) 2025/2083.

## Personal Luggage and Military Goods

FACT: "This Regulation shall not apply to: … (b) goods contained in the personal luggage of travellers coming from a third country provided that the intrinsic value of such goods does not exceed the value specified for goods of negligible value as referred to in Article 23 of Regulation (EC) No 1186/2009; (c) goods to be moved or used in the context of military activities pursuant to Article 1, point (49), of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2015/2446." (Article 2(3)(b) and (c).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 2(3)(b)(c). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Operational implication:* The military goods exemption may be relevant for humanitarian cargo operations if any goods are routed under military activity classifications. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether specific humanitarian shipments qualify for this exemption requires review against the Article 1(49) Delegated Regulation (EU) 2015/2446 definition on a case-by-case basis.

## Annex III Countries

FACT: CBAM does not apply to goods originating in Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, and Switzerland, nor to the territories of Büsingen, Heligoland, Livigno, Ceuta, and Melilla. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Annex III. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Operational implication:* For trade lanes including EEA/EFTA countries (Americas–Europe routes transiting Norway or Switzerland are common in air freight), goods originating in those countries are exempt. Non-preferential origin rules (Article 2(5)) determine origin; substantial transformation in a transit country may shift origin.

## Offshore Production Exemption (Post-2025/2083)

FACT: "Hydrogen and electricity generated entirely on the continental shelf or in the exclusive economic zone (EEZ) of an EEA member state and imported directly into the EU customs territory are exempt from CBAM." *Source: ICAP, EU adopts simplifications of CBAM rules ahead of the compliance phase starting in 2026, 20 October 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/eu-adopts-simplifications-cbam-rules-ahead-compliance-phase-starting-2026

*Operational implication:* This exemption is sector-specific (hydrogen and electricity only) and unlikely to affect the workspace's primary cargo verticals.

## Inward Processing and Returned Goods

FACT: "Where processed products resulting from an inward processing procedure as referred to in Article 256 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013 are imported, the authorised CBAM declarant shall report in the CBAM declaration the emissions embedded in the goods that were placed under the inward processing procedure and resulted in the imported processed products, even where the processed products are not goods listed in Annex I." (Article 6(3).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 6(3). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: "Where the imported goods listed in Annex I to this Regulation are processed products resulting from an outward processing procedure as referred to in Article 259 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013, the authorised CBAM declarant shall report in the CBAM declaration only the emissions of the processing operation undertaken outside the customs territory of the Union." (Article 6(4).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 6(4). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

FACT: "Where the imported goods are returned goods as referred to in Article 203 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013, the authorised CBAM declarant shall report separately, in the CBAM declaration, 'zero' for the total embedded emissions corresponding to those goods." (Article 6(5).) *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, Article 6(5). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956

*Operational implication:* For the workspace's fine art, luxury goods, and film and TV verticals, temporary import/export (ATA Carnets, outward processing) and return movements are common. The returned goods provision (zero-reporting) and the outward processing carve-out are operationally relevant for equipment and goods that travel out of the EU for production and return.

## OECD Quantitative Modelling (2025)

*Analytical inference (based on source listing only — full OECD report not reproduced in source blocks):* The OECD Science, Technology and Industry Working Paper on "Potential Effects of EU CBAM Along the Supply Chain" (2025) addresses compliance cost estimates, carbon leakage dynamics, and WTO-compatibility literature. This paper is listed as a corroborating source but its content is not reproduced in the source blocks. Specific quantitative findings cannot be stated as FACTs from the source content available. The paper is identified as a candidate for full retrieval and detailed review; its URL is listed in New Sources Identified.

The mechanism: CBAM raises the cost of importing carbon-intensive goods → producers in third countries either invest in decarbonisation or accept price premiums → importers face higher goods costs → freight demand patterns may shift toward lower-carbon-intensity origins over time. Effect on the workspace's transport volume in these sectors requires client-specific modelling.

claims.claim_text: [jurisdictional_scope] Goods shall be imported into the customs territory of the Union only by an authorised CBAM declarant.

[primary_deadline] The start of CBAM certificates sales is postponed from 1 January 2026 to 1 February 2027. Declarants will acquire certificates in 2027 for embedded emissions from 2026 imports, with full compliance obligations applicable retroactively. Certificates prices for the 2026 compliance year will reflect the quarterly average of 2026 EU ETS allowance prices.
[primary_deadline] The submission deadline for the annual CBAM declaration is postponed to 30 September (previously 31 May) of the year following importation of covered goods.

[penalty_summary] An authorised CBAM declarant who fails to surrender, by 31 May of each year, the number of CBAM certificates that corresponds to the emissions embedded in goods imported during the preceding calendar year shall be held liable for the payment of a penalty. Such a penalty shall be identical to the excess emissions penalty set out in Article 16(3) of Directive 2003/87/EC and increased pursuant to Article 16(4) of that Directive, applicable in the year of importation of the goods. Such a penalty shall apply for each CBAM certificate that the authorised CBAM declarant has not surrendered.
[penalty_summary] The payment of the penalty shall not release the authorised CBAM declarant from the obligation to surrender the outstanding number of CBAM certificates in a given year.
[effective_date] The definitive phase started 1 January 2026.
The first CBAM certificate price is now available — the Commission published the first quarterly price of CBAM certificates for 2026 Q1 on 7 April 2026.

[primary_deadline] By 31 May of each year, and for the first time in 2027 for the year 2026, each authorised CBAM declarant shall use the CBAM registry referred to in Article 14 to submit a CBAM declaration for the preceding calendar year.

[penalty_summary] Where a person other than an authorised CBAM declarant introduces goods into the customs territory of the Union without complying with the obligations under this Regulation, that person shall be held liable for the payment of a penalty … an amount from three to five times the penalty referred to in paragraph 1, applicable in the year of introduction of the goods, for each CBAM certificate that the person has not surrendered.

Commission welcomes Council agreement on strengthening CBAM — The European Commission welcomes the Council agreement to extend the CBAM to specific downstream goods and reinforce existing anti-circumvention safeguards (12 June 2026).
The Council agreement of 12 June 2026 on downstream goods extension indicates the scope of CBAM is expanding beyond the current six sectors.
New deadline announced for CBAM CCP Call for Tenders — The new deadline for submissions is Monday, 6 April 2026 at 5:00 PM (CET).

Technical Study on Indirect Emissions in the CBAM, including annexes, now available on the Publications Office of the European Union website was published 8 June 2026. The study addresses three questions: 1) how to determine operational default emission factors for indirect emissions; 2) under what conditions declarants should be allowed to claim actual indirect emissions… and 3) whether and how indirect emissions coverage could be extended to additional CBAM sectors.
[jurisdictional_scope] This Regulation applies to goods listed in Annex I originating in a third country.
Artificially splitting shipments into consignments the intrinsic value of which does not exceed the threshold referred to in Article 2(3) constitutes a practice of circumvention.
Imported goods shall be considered as originating in third countries in accordance with the rules for non-preferential origin as referred to in Article 59 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

The DG TAXUD legislation page lists a Proposal for a REGULATION OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL amending Regulation (EU) 2023/956 as regards the extension of its scope to downstream goods and anti-circumvention measures.

On 13 May 2026, the Commission published the implementing act for a 4-week public consultation ending on 10 June 2026. The publication follows a call for evidence whose synopsis report is also available.
The Common Central Platform through which CBAM certificates are sold is still in procurement as of early April 2026, with certificates sales starting February 2027.
This study is pre-legislative policy analysis feeding future implementing acts on indirect emissions.

This Regulation shall not apply to goods listed in Annex I to this Regulation which are imported into the customs territory of the Union provided that the intrinsic value of such goods does not exceed, per consignment, the value specified for goods of negligible value as referred to in Article 23 of Council Regulation (EC) No 1186/2009.
[jurisdictional_scope] This Regulation shall not apply to goods originating in Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, Switzerland and the territories of Büsingen, Heligoland, Livigno, Ceuta, Melilla.
For goods listed in Annex II only direct emissions shall be calculated and taken into account.

The CBAM declaration shall contain: (a) the total quantity of each type of goods imported during the preceding calendar year; (b) the total embedded emissions in the goods; (c) the total number of CBAM certificates to be surrendered; (d) copies of verification reports, issued by accredited verifiers.

The requirement for declarants to hold a number of CBAM certificates on their registry account at the end of each quarter is reduced to 50% of embedded emissions in goods imported since the beginning of the calendar year (down from 80%).
The Commission shall calculate the price of CBAM certificates as the average of the closing prices of EU ETS allowances on the auction platform for each calendar week.

The authorised CBAM declarant shall ensure that the total embedded emissions declared in the CBAM declaration submitted pursuant to Article 6 are verified by a verifier accredited pursuant to Article 18, based on the verification principles set out in Annex VI.

[primary_deadline] By 31 May of each year, and for the first time in 2027 for the year 2026, each authorised CBAM declarant shall use the CBAM registry referred to in Article 14 to submit a CBAM declaration for the preceding calendar year. As amended by Regulation (EU) 2025/2083, the deadline is 30 September.

[primary_deadline] By 31 May of each year, and for the first time in 2027 for the year 2026, the authorised CBAM declarant shall surrender via the CBAM registry a number of CBAM certificates that corresponds to the embedded emissions declared.
The authorised CBAM declarant shall ensure that the number of CBAM certificates on its account in the CBAM registry at the end of each quarter corresponds to at least 80 % of the embedded emissions.

Certificates prices for the 2026 compliance year will reflect the quarterly average of 2026 EU ETS allowance prices. Thereafter, CBAM certificates can be purchased any time and prices will reflect the weekly average of EU ETS auction closing prices.

Standard annual average carbon prices, or default carbon prices, may be used for countries where carbon pricing mechanisms are in place, but an actual carbon price paid cannot be established. From 2027, the Commission will publish default carbon prices and related calculation methodologies in the CBAM registry.
The documentation verifying carbon price paid shall be certified by a person that is independent from the authorised CBAM declarant and from the authorities of the country of origin.

The calculation of the embedded emissions takes into account an adjustment factor reflecting the sectoral free allocation of allowances that is still provided to EU producers (in line with the gradual phase-out of free allocation for CBAM-covered sectors in the EU ETS from 2026 to 2034).
Any person accredited in accordance with Implementing Regulation (EU) 2018/2067 for a relevant group of activities shall be an accredited verifier for the purpose of this Regulation.
Commissioning accredited verifiers for each producer installation in the supply chain is a prerequisite for the annual declaration.

The authorised CBAM declarant shall keep those records of information referred to in paragraph 5, including the report of the verifier, until the end of the fourth year after the year in which the CBAM declaration has been or should have been submitted.

Verification principles: verifiers shall carry out verifications with an attitude of professional scepticism; the total embedded emissions to be declared in the CBAM declaration shall be considered as verified only if the verifier finds with reasonable assurance that the verification report is free of material misstatements and of material non-conformities; installation visits by the verifier shall be mandatory except where specific criteria for waiving the installation visit are met.
The authorised CBAM declarant shall keep records of the information required to calculate the embedded emissions in accordance with the requirements laid down in Annex V.

An authorised CBAM declarant may claim in the CBAM declaration a reduction in the number of CBAM certificates to be surrendered in order to take into account the carbon price paid in the country of origin for the declared embedded emissions.
Carbon prices paid in a third country other than the country of origin of the imported goods are also eligible for deduction, if corresponding evidence can be provided.

The CBAM certificates to be surrendered in accordance with Article 22 of this Regulation shall be adjusted to reflect the extent to which EU ETS allowances are allocated free of charge in accordance with Article 10a of Directive 2003/87/EC to installations producing, within the Union, the goods listed in Annex I.

As free allocation phases out between 2026 and 2034, the adjustment factor decreases, meaning the effective number of CBAM certificates to be surrendered increases over time for the same volume of goods.
The repurchase price for each CBAM certificate shall be the price paid by the authorised CBAM declarant for that certificate at the time of purchase.
CN Chapter 72 (iron and steel) is covered under Annex I with specific exclusions for certain ferro-alloys (7202 2x) and ferrous waste/scrap (7204).
The application for an authorisation shall be submitted via the CBAM registry established in accordance with Article 14.
Access to the CBAM registry should be requested through the National Competent Authority (NCA) of the EU country in which the importer is established.
The number of certificates subject to repurchase shall be limited to one third of the total number of CBAM certificates purchased by the authorised CBAM declarant during the previous calendar year.
The authorised CBAM declarant shall submit the repurchase request by 30 June of each year during which CBAM certificates were surrendered.
Aluminium products should be included in the CBAM as they are highly exposed to carbon leakage. Moreover, in several industrial applications they are in direct competition with steel products.
[primary_deadline] First declaration for year 2026 is due by 30 September 2027 (as amended; original 31 May 2027).
The workspace may be engaged by clients to act as a delegated third party for CBAM declaration submission, without itself being the authorised CBAM declarant.
For every shipment the workspace handles as customs agent or indirect customs representative for EU imports, a CBAM Annex I screen should be applied at booking/classification stage.

A guarantee is required if the applicant was not established throughout the two financial years preceding the year when the application was submitted. The guarantee shall be a bank guarantee, payable at first demand, by a financial institution operating in the Union or another form of guarantee which provides equivalent assurance.

Authorized CBAM declarants may delegate submission of CBAM declarations to a third party, while retaining responsibility. The third party must be established in an EU member state and hold an Economic Operators Registration and Identification (EORI) number.
The Commission may review CBAM declarations within the period ending with the fourth year after the year during which the CBAM declarations should have been submitted.

Annex V minimum data requirements include: identification of the authorised CBAM declarant (name, CBAM account number); type and quantity of goods; country of origin; actual emissions or default values; and, for actual emissions, identification of the installation, contact information of the operator, verification reports, and specific embedded emissions.
Records must be retained until the end of the fourth year after the year in which the CBAM declaration has been or should have been submitted.
[jurisdictional_scope] CBAM does not apply to goods originating in Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, and Switzerland, nor to the territories of Büsingen, Heligoland, Livigno, Ceuta, and Melilla.

Where the imported goods listed in Annex I are processed products resulting from an outward processing procedure, the authorised CBAM declarant shall report in the CBAM declaration only the emissions of the processing operation undertaken outside the customs territory of the Union.

Where processed products resulting from an inward processing procedure are imported, the authorised CBAM declarant shall report in the CBAM declaration the emissions embedded in the goods that were placed under the inward processing procedure and resulted in the imported processed products, even where the processed products are not goods listed in Annex I.

OECD analysis of supply-chain effects is relevant to the workspace's freight forwarding clients in the iron/steel, aluminium, and fertiliser sectors who will face upstream cost increases as producers pass through CBAM costs.

This Regulation shall not apply to goods contained in the personal luggage of travellers coming from a third country provided that the intrinsic value of such goods does not exceed the value specified for goods of negligible value; and goods to be moved or used in the context of military activities pursuant to Article 1, point (49), of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2015/2446.

[effective_date] It shall apply from 1 October 2023. However: (a) Articles 5, 10, 14, 16 and 17 shall apply from 31 December 2024; (b) Article 2(2) and Articles 4, 6 to 9, 15 and 19, Article 20(1), (3), (4) and (5), Articles 21 to 27 and 31 shall apply from 1 January 2026.
